← the Atlas

Chat-Native Agents and kolu — have the cake, eat it too

Comparisons·seedling·proposed·JUN 24, 2026

Anthropic's Claude Tag (and Karpathy's read of it) moves coding into the chat. It threatens the half of kolu you stare at — the canvas — but not the half that matters. kolu's live-PTY substrate and the pulam awareness daemon are exactly what a manage-from-chat world needs and Slack lacks; the cake-and-eat-it bridge is one authenticated, model-agnostic ingress beside kaval. The wiring is cheap; the front door is the product.

Contents

  • The shift: the agent moves into the chat
  • kolu’s two halves: the cockpit erodes, the substrate is the same bet
  • The bridge: terminal-grade control, chat-grade reach
  • What stands in the way
  • The call: build the front door, not a chat app

A read of Anthropic’s Claude Tag (Claude joins Slack as a tagged teammate), its agent identity & access model, and Karpathy’s framing of it as “the 3rd major redesign of LLM UIUX” — held against what kolu actually is, and read alongside the Hacker News reaction. Grounded in a four-lens analysis (threat · bridge · moat · moves) with an adversarial red-team pass; the optimistic claims below are the ones that survived it.

Headline

The chat-native paradigm threatens the half of kolu you stare at — the canvas — not the half that matters. kolu’s live-PTY substrate (kaval) and its read-only awareness daemon (pulam) are exactly the org-wide telemetry a “work from Slack” world needs and chat doesn’t have. So kolu isn’t facing extinction; it’s at risk of shipping the wrong half. The cake-and-eat-it move is to keep terminal-grade control and gain chat-grade reach through one authenticated, model-agnostic ingress beside kaval — the only leg not already built. The wiring is cheap; the front door is the product.

The shift: the agent moves into the chat

Karpathy isn’t describing a chatbot you ask questions. He’s describing a coworker: a persistent agent that lives where the team already works, holds org-wide tools and context, and runs tasks on its own while you do something else. You @-tag it, walk away, and it pings you when it needs you.

It is a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools and context, working alongside teams of humans. … it’s writing the majority of code, it’s deeply integrated, multiplayer, and it starts to feel like everyone is a manager. … I work from Slack now.

— Andrej Karpathy

His own warning is the load-bearing part: it’s not “LLM Q&A with RAG over Slack.” The work itself moves into chat, and the human’s job shifts from driving keystrokes to managing agents. That’s the bet to react to.

The announcement fills in the shape: one shared Claude per channel (a teammate, not a bot-per-person), work it “schedule[s] … for itself” and pursues “over hours or days,” memory that carries across channels with permission, an admin audit log of every task and who asked for it, and per-channel tool and token limits — all on Opus 4.8. It’s Slack-only today, explicitly aiming to reach “the many other places they work,” and the proof-point is blunt: “65% of our product team’s code is created by our internal version.” This is the “it actually works” Karpathy meant — not a toy to wave off.

kolu’s two halves: the cockpit erodes, the substrate is the same bet

Be honest about the bear case first. The place you sit moves off the terminal. A manager who delegates and walks away has no use for an infinite canvas of live terminal tiles — panning, zooming, splitting. The canvas is kolu’s headline, demoable identity, and it’s exactly what a “work from Slack” user doesn’t want to look at. Worse, the one ergonomic Claude Tag sells — kick off a task from your phone — is the one thing kolu cannot do today: there’s no submit-from-outside, no remote trigger; an agent only runs if it’s live in a terminal inside kolu. If the center of gravity is async delegation, kolu’s current shape is a structural mismatch, not a missing button.

What an agent actually is (Andreessen) — and why it's already kolu's blueprint

Marc Andreessen, on what decades of agent research quietly converged on: “an agent is … a language model, and above that a bash shell … and then a file system, and the state is stored in files … markdown … plus cron,” and “every part of that other than the model is something we already completely know.” The kicker: “your agent is now actually independent of the model … you can swap out a different LLM underneath … all of the state stored in the files will be retained” — like “swapping out a chip and recompiling, but it’s still your agent with all its memories.” On protocols: “this whole idea where we need MCP and these fancy protocols — no, we just need a command-line thing.”

That is kolu’s architecture, line for line: the shell is the interface to every tool (kolu watches it, doesn’t wrap it), the files are the real intelligence (the Atlas is the markdown knowledgebase — a Zettelkasten knowledge graph, not chat exhaust), and the model is the swappable chip (claude · codex · opencode). Claude Tag inverts all three — model fixed (Opus 4.8), shell and files hidden inside a cloud sandbox, memory as lossy Slack scrollback. kolu keeps the durable substance — shell, files, your own infra — and lets the chip swap. It’s the same simplicity that made the web (HTTP, JSON, human-readable text), arriving for agents.

Now the other half, which the bear case misses:

  • The terminal isn’t nostalgia — it’s a capability chat doesn’t have. Chat is async text; it is not a live PTY. The moment a task needs a human to drop to bash, answer an interactive prompt, or watch raw streaming output, the terminal is the only surface that works. Chat hides that gap; it doesn’t close it. As one HN commenter put it, “you don’t tend to have long sessions with agents in Slack like you would in the terminal” — chat suits short, fire-and-forget delegations; the deep, interactive work still wants a terminal.
  • Agent-agnosticism is a structural moat, not a feature. kolu runs claude, codex, opencode, or anything in a shell and reads its state with no per-agent wrapping. Claude Tag is single-vendor by construction — Anthropic will never ship a console that ranks a competitor’s agent next to its own. An org running several agents cannot get one neutral “who needs me” board from Anthropic.
  • kolu already built the manager’s dashboard. The awareness daemon pulam serves “what is every agent doing” as one live surface, and three read-only mirrors (the Dock plus the TUI and web fleet boards) read one ranking and stay in lockstep. Slack gives you a thread per task; it does not give you a ranked, needs-you-first board of every agent’s live state.
  • The other hard problem is a real isolated environment — and kolu runs on your own. The sharpest line in the HN thread: “the hard problem is giving a shared agent durable organizational memory and a real isolated environment.” Anthropic’s answer is a service account inside its cloud; kolu’s is a real PTY on a host you control. kolu’s remote-terminals work is heading toward running agents on sandboxed hosts — an Incus cluster, say — your own isolated infra, not a vendor’s opaque sandbox. (On the roadmap; kolu is open source, so the integration point is in the open.)
  • Durable memory that isn’t lossy Slack scrollback. The other half of “org memory” is the rear-facing brain, and HN’s verdict on keeping it in chat is brutal: “Slack as a memory store is lossy,” “75% of Slack shouldn’t be remembered.” kolu already has the opposite — the Atlas: a git-versioned, PR-reviewed, agent-cheap markdown knowledgebase (this very note lives in it). A curated second-brain, not chat exhaust.

The liability isn’t the product — it’s narrating the canvas as the product. The canvas is the half a manager walks away from; the substrate is the half that wins.

The bridge: terminal-grade control, chat-grade reach

kolu already has the async awareness shipped. What it lacks is async action. The whole bridge is the asymmetry in this one picture:

The smallest end-to-end loop:

  1. From any chat: “@kolu run claude ‘fix the failing auth test’ on repo/branch” → spawns a real kolu terminal on your server.
  2. The agent works in a live tile; state and activity are tracked exactly as today.
  3. It blocks on you (awaiting_user) → the existing alert fold fires → a new sink posts to chat with the screen tail and approve/deny buttons.
  4. You tap approve on your phone → a single keystroke (y⏎) is written into that live PTY.
  5. The agent finishes → the tile parks to a sleeping terminal; the final diff is posted back to the thread.

Every leg but one reuses something already built:

Leg Reuses Status
spawn an agent · write y⏎ kaval already exposes the full write path (spawn · write bytes · kill · resize) — kolu’s own tiles call it shipped
forward across hosts the mirror path already drives a remote action over browser → ssh → host with no kolu-specific code shipped
ping out when blocked the awaiting_user → alert fold that already pulses the tile, toasts, and fires an OS notification shipped
park + resume sleeping terminals, survive a full restart shipped
submit / approve from chat — nothing — to build

The one missing leg is an authenticated ingress: a small daemon beside kaval (the PTY owner), not inside pulam (the read-only observer), exposing exactly two verbs — submit (spawn an agent with a given argv) and approve/reply (write bytes into a named live tile) — behind a real front door: per-user identity, and default-deny on which argv may run and which tiles are writable. Per your steer, it’s one generic messaging seam — Slack, Discord, WhatsApp, Telegram behind a single adapter — not a Slack-shaped wrapper, so the agent-agnostic moat extends to the transport too.

Two things the red-team insisted we not soft-pedal

The leg is not a tenth of the work. The plumbing — bytes can move — is cheap and done. The front door is most of the value and all of the risk: spawn / write is full remote code execution on your box, over a surface that is unauthenticated today (gated only by same-origin + socket permissions). The auth model is the project — and it’s exactly the “heavy under-the-hood security work” Karpathy names as the gap between a hackathon demo and a product. Anthropic just shipped a whole agent identity & access model for precisely this — channel-scoped service identities, default-deny tool and network-egress allowlists, per-action audit, least-privilege, and “just-in-time” approval of a single sensitive action — the strongest possible proof the door is the work. It also hands kolu the spec to mirror, and the JIT “approve one action in the moment” grant is the approve-from-phone gate above; kolu’s edge is doing it self-hosted, on infra you own, not in a vendor’s console.

The honest unit of chat control is approve / deny / one-line reply at a blocked gate — not “steer.” Raw bytes are perfect for y⏎; they are a bad way to drive a full-screen TUI’s arrow keys through a chat box. Don’t promise interactive driving from Slack.

What stands in the way

  • Self-hosted, single-user today. A moat for the solo / own-iron / no-procurement crowd; a structural disqualifier for the enterprise-cloud deployments Karpathy points at (no SSO, no audit, no central control). Defensible and small can both be true.
  • No offline — the live WebSocket is the app. Push-out (the ping) is free; reply-in needs a new always-listening authenticated endpoint your phone reaches. It cannot be a background service-worker reply.
  • No submit-from-outside today. This is the single fatal gap for the chat-delegation thesis. Unshipped — not “nearly there.”
  • Remote multi-host is in flight, not shipped (see remote terminals). A chat ingress today is single-host — the box pulam/kaval already run on. Fleet-wide ingress waits on the remote-host work.
  • Multiplayer is essentially zero. The awareness read is shared; real multiplayer (identity, who-claimed-what, two humans on one tile) has no primitives yet and is a separate epic — and a chat ingress implicitly invites multiple taggers, so the auth leg must answer “who is this” too.
  • Stay vendor-neutral. A model-agnostic ingress (any argv) and a neutral fleet board — never a Claude-shaped or Slack-shaped wrapper, which would spend the one structural moat kolu has. It’s HN’s loudest refrain too — model-agnosticism and token-cost control as the moat, and Slack-only as a “moat-killer” (Anthropic itself plans to expand past Slack). So chat-surface breadth is table stakes; the substrate is the differentiation.

The call: build the front door, not a chat app

kolu is not threatened with extinction — it’s at risk of shipping the wrong half. The canvas-as-cockpit erodes in a manage-from-chat world; the awareness daemon and the live-PTY substrate are exactly what that world needs and Slack lacks. kolu is complementary today and positioned to win the neutral console — the one place an org sees claude, codex, and opencode ranked side by side, with real terminals underneath, on infrastructure it controls (down to sandboxed hosts like an Incus cluster).

The single highest-leverage move is not a Slack app. It’s the authenticated, default-deny, model-agnostic ingress beside kaval that turns the read-only fleet board into a delegate-and-approve surface, with a phone-friendly version of the existing web board as its face. Sequence it behind the remote-host work, scope it to single-user, and ship the approve-from-phone loop first. And resist the temptation to call the front door a one-liner: the wiring is cheap; the door is the product.

Beyond the bridge — the forward-facing opening

The most-upvoted “killer feature” in the thread isn’t chat-as-IDE; it’s an auto-assembling, forward-facing punchlist — discussion decomposed into subtasks, blocking relationships tracked, specs and facts filed into the right task: “Company Brain / Knowledgebase is rear-facing, whereas todo is future-facing.” kolu already owns both ends of that axis: pulam is the present tense (“what is every agent doing”), and the Atlas is the rear-facing brain. The gap — and the opening — is the future tense: a task/punchlist layer the awareness substrate and the chat ingress could feed. Out of scope for the bridge itself, but the natural place it grows next.
